title: Stdlib: .ri prelude loader — include_str!, parse, compile, cache
analysis:
The task builds infrastructure to load stdlib .ri files embedded via include_str!() in reify-compiler, parse+compile them once, cache with OnceLock, and make their definitions (traits, enums, functions) available to user code without explicit import.

Existing state:
- crates/reify-compiler/stdlib/materials_mechanical.ri already exists with 9 traits + 1 enum (Material, Elastic, Strong, Hard, FatigueRated, FractureTough, Ductile, ImpactResistant, Damping, HardnessScale)
- Existing tests in materials_mechanical_tests.rs load it from filesystem via env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR") — not embedded
- No include_str!() for .ri files anywhere; no OnceLock/lazy_static in the codebase
- module_dag.rs has ModuleResolver with std.* prefix convention but isn't wired to compilation
- compile() is a single-module function that builds a trait_registry HashMap from the module's own trait declarations (line 2094-2098). Trait conformance checking (check_trait_conformance) uses this registry.
- Engine::eval() takes a single &CompiledModule, stores module.functions in self.functions for expression evaluation. No multi-module merge exists.
- reify-eval already depends on reify-compiler (Cargo.toml), so calling into compiler's stdlib_loader is fine.

Key challenge: Trait conformance is a compile-time concern (trait_registry in compile()), while function availability is an eval-time concern (EvalContext.functions). The prelude must serve both:
1. compile_with_prelude() seeds trait_registry + enum resolution with prelude definitions
2. Engine::eval() merges prelude functions into self.functions for expression evaluation

Approach: Create stdlib_loader module with OnceLock caching, add compile_with_prelude() to the compiler that seeds the compilation context with prelude definitions, and modify Engine to store prelude and merge definitions during eval().

prerequisites.description: Verify materials_mechanical.ri exists and compiles cleanly with existing tests (cargo test -p reify-compiler --test materials_mechanical_tests)

steps.description: Write failing tests in crates/reify-compiler/tests/stdlib_loader_tests.rs: (1) load_stdlib() returns non-empty slice, (2) all modules have zero error-level diagnostics, (3) materials_mechanical.ri traits are present (Material, Elastic, Strong etc.), (4) second call returns same pointer (cached). Tests fail because stdlib_loader module doesn't exist yet.

Create crates/reify-compiler/src/stdlib_loader.rs: static STDLIB_CACHE: OnceLock<Vec<CompiledModule>>; pub fn load_stdlib() -> &'static [CompiledModule] using get_or_init that include_str!s each .ri file from ../stdlib/, parses with reify_syntax::parse, compiles with crate::compile, collects into Vec. Add 'pub mod stdlib_loader;' to lib.rs. All step-1 tests should pass.

Write failing test in stdlib_loader_tests.rs: compile_with_prelude() makes prelude traits visible to user code. Test compiles 'structure def Steel : Elastic { param youngs_modulus: Real = 200.0  param poissons_ratio: Real = 0.3  param shear_modulus: Real = 77.0 }' using compile_with_prelude(parsed, load_stdlib()) and asserts zero error diagnostics and trait_bounds contains 'Elastic'. Fails because compile_with_prelude doesn't exist.

Add pub fn compile_with_prelude(parsed: &ParsedModule, prelude: &[CompiledModule]) -> CompiledModule to lib.rs. Refactor compile() to delegate: compile(parsed) calls compile_with_prelude(parsed, &[]). In compile_with_prelude: (1) collect prelude trait_defs into Vec<&CompiledTrait>, prelude enum_defs into Vec<EnumDef>, prelude functions into Vec<CompiledFunction>, (2) build resolution_enums = prelude_enums + module enums for passing to compile_trait/compile_field/compile_entity, (3) build trait_registry from prelude_traits first then module traits (module overrides prelude on name collision), (4) pass resolution_enums and combined trait_registry to internal compilation, (5) output CompiledModule contains only module-local definitions. Step-3 test passes.

Write failing test in stdlib_loader_tests.rs: compile_with_prelude injects trait constraint defaults. Compile 'structure def Steel : Strong { param yield_strength: Real = 250.0  param uts: Real = 400.0  param compressive_strength: Real = 250.0 }' with prelude, assert template.constraints is non-empty (uts >= yield_strength from Strong trait default). Should pass if step-4 correctly wires trait_registry.

Fix any issues from step-5 test. Verify that prelude trait defaults (constraints, params, lets) are correctly injected into conforming structures during compile_with_prelude. This may require ensuring resolution_enums is passed to check_trait_conformance's internal compile_constraint_expr calls.

Write failing test in crates/reify-eval/tests/stdlib_prelude_tests.rs: (1) Engine::new() stores prelude — engine has non-empty prelude field, (2) eval() with a user module compiled via compile_with_prelude works correctly for a structure conforming to a prelude trait — values are populated, no error diagnostics. Fails because Engine doesn't have prelude field yet.

Modify Engine in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs: (1) add field 'prelude: &'static [CompiledModule]' to Engine struct, (2) in Engine::new(), set prelude: reify_compiler::stdlib_loader::load_stdlib(), (3) in eval(), after self.functions = module.functions.clone(), extend self.functions with prelude functions: for pm in self.prelude { self.functions.extend(pm.functions.iter().cloned()); }. Add pub fn prelude(&self) -> &[CompiledModule] accessor for test visibility. Step-7 tests pass.

Write end-to-end test in stdlib_prelude_tests.rs: full pipeline from .ri source string through compile_with_prelude + Engine::eval. User code 'structure def Steel : Material + Elastic { param density: Real = 7800.0  param name: String = "A36"  param youngs_modulus: Real = 200.0  param poissons_ratio: Real = 0.3  param shear_modulus: Real = 77.0 }' compiles and evaluates successfully. Assert: (1) no error diagnostics from compilation, (2) eval returns values for all 5 params, (3) trait_bounds on template include both Material and Elastic.

Fix any remaining issues to make step-9 end-to-end test pass. Verify backward compatibility: run existing test suites for reify-compiler and reify-eval to ensure no regressions from the compile() refactoring or Engine prelude addition.

design_decisions.decision: Use std::sync::OnceLock for stdlib caching instead of lazy_static or once_cell
design_decisions.rationale: OnceLock is in std since Rust 1.70 — no extra dependency needed. The workspace already uses edition 2024. OnceLock provides the exact semantics needed: initialize-once, thread-safe, zero-cost after init.

design_decisions.decision: include_str!() for embedding .ri files rather than filesystem loading
design_decisions.rationale: Compile-time embedding ensures stdlib is always available regardless of install location. Eliminates filesystem errors at runtime. The .ri files are small (< 5KB each), so binary size impact is negligible. This is the same pattern used by reify-mcp for documentation chunks.

design_decisions.decision: compile_with_prelude() seeds trait_registry and enum resolution context rather than merging parsed declarations
design_decisions.rationale: Merging at the compile level (seeding trait_registry HashMap) is cleaner than merging ParsedModule declarations because: (1) prelude is already compiled, avoiding redundant parsing, (2) compiled traits have resolved types, (3) output CompiledModule only contains user definitions, avoiding double-counting. The alternative of merging parsed declarations would require converting CompiledTrait back to TraitDecl or maintaining both representations.

design_decisions.decision: compile() delegates to compile_with_prelude(parsed, &[]) — no code duplication
design_decisions.rationale: The existing compile() function becomes a thin wrapper. All compilation logic lives in compile_with_prelude(). This ensures backward compatibility — all existing callers of compile() continue to work unchanged, while new callers can opt into prelude visibility.

design_decisions.decision: Engine always loads prelude in new() rather than opt-in
design_decisions.rationale: The task specifies 'Engine::new() merges stdlib modules into eval context'. Since OnceLock makes the second call effectively free (<1ms), the cost of always loading is negligible. The current stdlib (materials_mechanical.ri) only contains traits and enums — no functions or templates — so existing eval tests are unaffected. The prelude is stored as &'static [CompiledModule] (a borrowed reference to the OnceLock cache), costing only a pointer-width field.

design_decisions.decision: Prelude definitions excluded from output CompiledModule in compile_with_prelude
design_decisions.rationale: The output module should only reflect what the user wrote. Prelude definitions are used as compilation context (trait_registry, type resolution) but not included in the output's trait_defs, enum_defs, or functions vectors. This prevents double-counting when multiple modules are compiled and avoids content_hash changes from prelude updates affecting user module hashes.

reuse.what: Existing stdlib/materials_mechanical.ri file and test patterns
reuse.how: The .ri file is already validated by comprehensive tests. Reuse the file as-is for include_str!. Reuse test patterns (load_stdlib_module helper, error filtering, trait assertion style) as templates for stdlib_loader_tests.rs.

reuse.what: reify-mcp include_str! pattern for embedded content
reuse.how: Follow the same pattern of const CONTENT: &str = include_str!("path") for embedding .ri source files. Validates this is an established project pattern.

reuse.what: compile() internal trait_registry and enum_defs compilation context
reuse.how: compile_with_prelude extends the existing trait_registry HashMap and enum_defs Vec with prelude entries before passing to compile_entity, compile_trait, compile_field. The internal function signatures don't change — they already accept &HashMap<String, &CompiledTrait> and &[EnumDef].
Engine tests in stdlib_prelude_tests.rs use MockConstraintChecker::new() to create Engine instances, following the established eval test pattern.

reuse.what: Standard parse → compile → eval test pattern
reuse.how: End-to-end tests follow the established pattern: parse source string → compile → Engine::new(MockChecker) → engine.eval(&compiled) → assert values and diagnostics. The new tests add compile_with_prelude into this pipeline.
